points.text: Chiesi Group and Arbor Biotechnologies announced that ABO-101, an investigational gene editing therapy, received Orphan Drug Designation (ODD) from the European Commission for primary hyperoxaluria type 1 (PH1).
ABO-101 is designed to address the underlying disease biology of PH1 by reducing hepatic oxalate production through gene editing mechanisms.
The Phase 1/2 redePHine clinical study for ABO-101 is scheduled to be highlighted at the 15th International Hyperoxaluria Workshop on June 26, 2026.
Scientists utilized a newer, more precise form of CRISPR in early human embryos to investigate genes active during early human development stages.
Research published in Nature indicates base editing, a CRISPR variant, is gentler than earlier versions and avoids chromosome damage associated with previous CRISPR iterations.
CRISPR base editing trials in human embryos resulted in frequent mosaicism, characterized by a mixture of edited and unedited cells within the same embryo.

Researchers utilized next-generation CRISPR base editing tools on donated human embryos to investigate genes orchestrating early human development, demonstrating higher precision and lower destructiveness than earlier CRISPR iterations.
A study published in Nature confirmed that human embryos can tolerate CRISPR base editing and develop to a stage suitable for uterine implantation, though edits were not consistent across all cells.
Dieter Egli's lab at Columbia University reported a new preprint on base editing of human embryos, advancing CRISPR gene-editing research.

Chiesi Group / Arbor Biotechnologies — ABO-101 — Primary Hyperoxaluria Type 1 — European Commission granted Orphan Drug Designation; program utilizes gene editing to reduce hepatic oxalate production.

Chiesi Group / Arbor Biotechnologies — ABO-101 — Primary Hyperoxaluria Type 1 — European Commission granted Orphan Drug Designation; Phase 1/2 redePHine study data to be presented at 15th International Hyperoxaluria Workshop on June 26, 2026.

Unspecified Research Group — CRISPR Base Editing — Early Human Development — Published in Nature; demonstrated reduced chromosome damage compared to earlier CRISPR versions but resulted in frequent embryonic mosaicism.

Unspecified Research Consortium — CRISPR Base Editing — Human Embryo Development — Published in Nature; demonstrated ability to edit embryos to implantation stage with improved accuracy over prior CRISPR forms, but resulted in mosaic cell populations with inconsistent edits.

Columbia University — Base editing of human embryos — Heritable gene editing — New preprint data released by Dieter Egli's lab; bioethicists call for temporary moratorium on reproductive applications.
Arbor Biotechnologies — Gene editing therapy commercialization — ABO-101 — Partnered with Chiesi Group (collaboration established 2025); advancing Phase 1/2 redePHine trial for PH1.
Chiesi Group — Rare disease gene editing expansion — ABO-101 — Secured EU Orphan Drug Designation for PH1 indication; leveraging global strategic collaboration with Arbor Biotechnologies.

Arbor Biotechnologies — Gene editing therapy commercialization — ABO-101 (PH1) — Strategic collaboration with Chiesi Group established 2025; received EC Orphan Drug Designation and US Rare Pediatric Disease Designation.

Arbor Biotechnologies — Gene editing therapy commercialization — ABO-101 (PH1) — Strategic collaboration with Chiesi Group established 2025; advancing next-generation genetic medicines for rare diseases.

ABO-101 — Mechanism: Gene editing to reduce hepatic oxalate production — Phase: 1/2 (redePHine) — Population: Primary Hyperoxaluria Type 1 (PH1) — Comparator: Not specified — Status: ODD granted by EC; data presentation planned for June 26, 2026 workshop.

ABO-101 — Mechanism: Gene editing therapy reducing hepatic oxalate production — Phase: 1/2 (redePHine) — Population: Primary Hyperoxaluria Type 1 (PH1) patients — Endpoints: Reduction in oxalate production to prevent kidney stones and failure.

CRISPR Base Editing — Mechanism: Precise nucleotide substitution without double-strand breaks — Phase: Preclinical (Human Embryo Research) — Population: Early-stage human embryos — Comparator: Earlier CRISPR versions — Results: Avoided chromosome damage but induced frequent mosaicism (mixed edited/unedited cells).

CRISPR Base Editing — Mechanism: Advanced genome editing with reduced off-target effects compared to standard CRISPR — Phase: Preclinical (Basic Science) — Population: Donated human embryos — Results: Embryos developed to implantation stage; edits resulted in mosaic cell populations (hodgepodge of altered and unaltered cells).

Human embryo base editing — Mechanism: CRISPR-based precise genome modification — Phase: Preclinical (preprint) — Population: Pre-implantation human embryos — Context: Study highlights ethical boundaries and calls for moratorium on heritable editing.

CRISPR Base Editing — Mechanism: Precise gene modification without double-strand breaks — Phase: Preclinical (early human embryo research) — Population: Early human embryos — Results: Avoided chromosome damage seen in earlier CRISPR versions; frequent mosaicism observed with mix of edited and unedited cells.

CRISPR Base Editing — Mechanism: Next-generation genome editing targeting specific DNA bases without double-strand breaks — Phase: Preclinical (Human Embryo Research) — Population: Donated human embryos — Results: Successful development to implantation stage; inconsistent editing across all cells creating mosaicism; reduced off-target destruction compared to earlier CRISPR forms.

Chiesi Group / Arbor Biotechnologies — Global strategic collaboration — ABO-101 — Partnership established in 2025 to develop and commercialize gene editing therapies for rare diseases; EU ODD granted June 25, 2026.

Chiesi Group / Arbor Biotechnologies — Global strategic collaboration — ABO-101 — Established 2025 to develop and commercialize gene editing therapies for rare diseases; EC Orphan Drug Designation secured for PH1 indication.

Chiesi Group / Arbor Biotechnologies — Global strategic collaboration — ABO-101 — Partnership established 2025 to develop and commercialize gene editing therapies for rare diseases; EC ODD secures regulatory support for development lifecycle.
